---
title: "Overview"
image: ../../man/figures/vignette-llm-assisted-tips.svg
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
---

{{< include _setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _pages-nav.qmd >}}

A short set of working principles I've converged on after a stretch of building R packages, dashboards, and analyses with Claude Code as a daily collaborator. None of these are novel — they're cribbed and consolidated from people much further along — but they're the ones that actually changed how I work.

## Three load-bearing principles

::: {.callout-important}
Before you read further: LLM-assisted development only works if you bake in **reproducibility**, **consistency**, and **validation** from day one. These are not optional extras to add later.

- **Reproducibility:** Every output is regenerated from locked source (git + Nix/pip lockfiles). No hand-tweaked CSV exports, no manual edits to deployed assets.
- **Consistency:** Code style, naming, structure, and test style are enforced mechanically (linters, formatters, pre-commit hooks). An LLM builds to a spec faster when the spec is non-negotiable.
- **Validation:** Every claim is testable. Tests drive the spec; the spec drives the code. If you can't write a test for it, you don't ship it.

The rest of this site and the [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/JohnGavin/llm/blob/main/AGENTS.md) file at the repo root detail these practices. Start there if you're new to this workflow.
:::

## The shape of the day

**Plan more than you execute. 60% planning, 40% execution.**

Counter-intuitive at first because LLMs are so fast you assume more execution = more output. In practice the opposite holds: an LLM can implement a clear spec in minutes but struggles for hours against a fuzzy one. Time spent sharpening the spec pays back several times over, because the fast part stays fast and the slow part (rework, blind alleys, conflicting context) shrinks.

The pattern that works for me:

1. **Sketch what you want.** WHAT, not HOW. Success criteria, not step-by-step instructions.
2. **Hand the sketch to a fresh LLM and ask it to tear the spec apart.** Identify points that are underspecified, inconsistent, or weird. Iterate until the remaining questions are genuinely niche.
3. **Hand the sharpened plan to a new LLM with a simple prompt.** "Implement this." If you've done step 2 well, the simple prompt is enough.

The rationale for separating step 2 from step 3 is that the LLM doing the critique should not be the one doing the build — different framings, different blind spots.
